title: New Orleans beignets
subtitle: Deep-fried yeasted squares with icing sugar
excerpt: New Orleans beignets: square pieces of soft yeasted dough fried until they puff dramatically and colour to a pale gold, then buried under a snowfall of icing sugar. Lighter than doughnuts, with an irregular hollow interior and a fragile, crisp shell.
sourceNotes: New Orleans beignets are a product of French colonial influence in Louisiana, derived from the French beignet (fried dough) tradition. The New Orleans version, served with café au lait, is specifically associated with the Café du Monde in New Orleans from 1862, though fried dough preparations appear in Louisiana cookbooks from the early nineteenth century. The recipe is reproduced in numerous Louisiana and Southern regional cookbooks and is in the public domain as a traditional preparation.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be made and proved, then refrigerated overnight. Cut and fry directly from the fridge the following morning. Do not refrigerate cut, proved beignets — they will over-proof.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Deep-fried at 180°C. Higher than doughnuts because beignets are thinner and need to puff fast. The dough self-basters as it puffs; turn continuously to colour evenly.

glossaryTerms.definition: As a beignet fries, the expanding steam inside the dough causes it to balloon rapidly and flip itself over in the oil. This is normal — the beignet seals the puffed interior and turns unaided. Turning it gently before it puffs will deflate it.

content.text: New Orleans beignets are squarish, not round, and the dough is rolled thinner than a doughnut. The
as each one hits the oil is the sign that the prove and temperature are right. Use enough icing sugar to cover them completely — this is not optional garnish, it is part of the eating experience.

Combine the flour, sugar, salt, and yeast. Add the evaporated milk, water, egg, and melted butter and mix until a soft, cohesive dough forms. Knead for 5–7 minutes until smooth. The dough will be slightly sticky — resist adding more flour. Place in an oiled bowl, cover, and prove for 1 hour until doubled.

Heat the oil to 180°C. On a lightly floured surface, roll the proved dough to 5 mm thick. Cut into 6–7 cm squares. Do not prove again after cutting — the beignets go straight into the oil. Fry 3–4 at a time for 45–60 seconds per side. They will puff dramatically and may flip themselves; help any that haven't turned by gently nudging them. Fry until pale gold on both sides. Drain briefly on the rack, then dust immediately and heavily with icing sugar.
